source.title: Northstar Bank x Redwood  Private Security Review (Audit Logs)
next_steps: Redwood to send final Confluence-derived requirements summary (PDF)
Northstar to provide Role ARN + ExternalId and target bucket prefix
Schedule follow-up technical deep dive on installer configuration
source.transcript:
Meeting header
Date: 2025-02-14
Time: 1:00 PM PT
Duration: 46 minutes
Attendees: Ava Chen (Redwood), Diego Fernandez (Redwood), Aisha Rahman (Redwood), Morgan Lee (Northstar), Samir Gupta (Northstar), Priya Desai (Northstar)

Summary (auto)
Customer requested details on exporting Redwood Private audit logs to customer-owned S3 including auth model, encryption, and whether log content includes prompts.

Topics (auto)
- Audit log event schema
- S3 bucket policy / permissions
- Encryption (SSE-KMS)
- Delivery semantics and buffering

Action items (auto)
- Redwood: Provide sample IAM role policy (prefix-scoped) and confirm no ListBucket requirement
- Customer: Provide KMS key requirements and desired retention period

Transcript
[00:00] Ava Chen: cool, thanks everyone. today we wanted to focus just on audit logs for the Private deployment.
[00:18] Morgan Lee: yep. we need to prove we can get audit events out of the cluster into our AWS account. retention is 1 year minimum.
[00:35] Diego Fernandez: makes sense. the current plan is an in-cluster exporter that writes JSON lines into S3 under a deterministic prefix.
[00:49] Samir Gupta: what does deterministic mean here?
[01:01] Diego Fernandez: like `redwood-audit/v1/tenant_id=.../cluster_id=.../date=YYYY-MM-DD/part-000001.jsonl.gz`. easy to lifecycle and ingest.
[01:20] Priya Desai: and the events include what exactly?
[01:27] Aisha Rahman: admin + control plane actions. actor, action, resource, result, request_id. importantly no prompt or completion payloads in audit logs.
[01:44] Morgan Lee: ok good. auth is the big concern. we do not allow access keys.
[01:56] Diego Fernandez: same  were using IRSA in the EKS cluster to assume a role in your account. short-lived creds. we require ExternalId.
[02:18] Samir Gupta: do you need ListBucket? our bucket policies are strict.
[02:24] Diego Fernandez: no. we can do just PutObject. if we use multipart we might need AbortMultipartUpload, but not list.
[02:38] Aisha Rahman: from security side, were going to fail closed if ExternalId isnt set. no silent fallback to keys.
[03:02] Morgan Lee: encryption?
[03:05] Diego Fernandez: supports SSE-S3 and SSE-KMS. you can provide your KMS key ARN.
[03:17] Priya Desai: do you support GCS too? we have a secondary environment.
[03:24] Diego Fernandez: yes in the same exporter were adding GCS via Workload Identity Federation.
[03:40] Morgan Lee: ok, timeline?
[03:44] Ava Chen: were doing a design partner rollout this month. well coordinate enablement and testing with you.
[04:02] Samir Gupta: delivery semantics  any gaps?
[04:08] Diego Fernandez: at-least-once. we buffer locally on disk; if S3 is down we pause consumption once buffer hits a watermark. youll have metrics like last success timestamp and buffer size.
[04:29] Morgan Lee: ok. can you send the formal requirements in writing?
[04:33] Aisha Rahman: yes. we have an internal requirements page and we can share a customer-friendly summary + example policy.
[44:50] Ava Chen: recap: well send IAM + KMS examples, youll share role arn/external id and prefix, and well schedule a follow-up on the installer config. sound good?
[45:05] Morgan Lee: yep.

source.title: Northstar Bank — Runtime LTS technical deep dive (cadence, backports, perf gates, artifacts)
source.summary: Northstar platform/SRE/security team asked for concrete Runtime LTS commitments: 12-month support window expectations, monthly patch cadence + out-of-band security hotfixes, backport eligibility and SLA, performance regression gating and waiver handling, and how Redwood provides verifiable artifacts (checksums/signatures/provenance/SBOM) for Private deployments. Redwood outlined proposed runtime-1.18-lts branch model, risk rubric for backports, perf canary gates (p50/p95 latency + throughput), and artifact verification workflow tied to commits and release notes. Customer requested written language for vendor risk and a sample signed artifact + SBOM.
topics: What LTS means (branching, support window, and what is/ isn't backported)
Patch cadence and emergency security patch procedure
Backport eligibility, approval workflow, and SLA targets
Performance stability: benchmark suite, thresholds, noisy results, waiver process
Private deployment requirements: version pinning, rollback, and change control
Artifact verification: provenance, checksums, signing keys, SBOM expectations
Release notes format: slower-change notes, known issues, compatibility matrix
questions: Can we stay on a runtime baseline for 12 months without forced upgrades?
What is the SLA to get a Sev1 crash fix into LTS vs mainline?
If perf regresses, do you guarantee rollback guidance and a point fix?
How do we verify an image is the exact one you claim (signatures, digests, SBOM, mapping to commit)?
Can we pin to a specific patch in Private (Helm/installer) and roll back without pulling new deps?
next_steps: Redwood to share draft Runtime LTS policy language (support window, cadence, exclusions) for Northstar vendor risk review.
Redwood to provide a sample LTS artifact bundle: image digest, checksums, signature verification steps, and SBOM/provenance example.
Northstar to send their internal change-control requirements (CAB timing, blackout windows) and their definition of Sev1/Sev2 for backport SLA alignment.
Schedule a follow-up with Northstar’s compliance lead to review signing key management and audit evidence expectations.
source.transcript:
Meeting Header
Date: 2025-02-06
Start time: 3:00 PM ET
Duration: 55 min
Call type: Technical deep dive
Customer: Northstar Bank

Attendees
Redwood Inference: Avery Johnson (AE), Connor O’Brien (Release Eng / LTS lead), Sean Gallagher (SRE), Sahana Iyer (CI/Build)
Northstar Bank: Priya Nair (Platform Eng), Michael Chen (SRE), Elena Petrova (Security Eng), Jordan Miles (Vendor Risk/Procurement)

---

[00:00:05] Avery Johnson: Alright, I think we’re recording. Thanks everyone for making time. Goal today is to go deep on runtime LTS expectations. Connor’s leading the LTS work, Sean from SRE, and Sahana on the build and artifact side.

[00:00:23] Priya Nair: Great. Just to level set, we’re evaluating Private plus Dedicated for a couple lines of business. But our biggest blocker is operational churn. We can’t do, you know, surprise runtime changes every few weeks.

[00:00:38] Connor O'Brien: Yep, makes sense. The LTS channel is designed specifically for that—slower-change, predictable patch cadence, and very constrained backports.

[00:00:49] Michael Chen: I’ll be direct: we need something we can pin for like a year. Not forever, but 12 months is the internal target for platform baselines.

[00:01:00] Connor O'Brien: Understood. What we’re proposing is a named LTS line, for example runtime-1.18-lts, with a defined support window—targeting 12 months of maintenance from branch cut. During that window, we do patch releases, not feature releases.

[00:01:18] Jordan Miles: When you say “maintenance,” that includes security patches, right? Like CVEs.

[00:01:23] Sean Gallagher: Yes. Security and critical stability fixes. But not, like, new batching strategies or scheduler rewrites.

[00:01:31] Elena Petrova: We’ll need clarity on what “critical” means. That’s where vendors get squishy.

[00:01:37] Connor O'Brien: Totally fair. For LTS, our backport rubric is basically three buckets: security fixes, Sev1 production incidents, and a smaller set of Sev2 if they’re high-impact and low-risk to backport.

[00:01:54] Priya Nair: Can you define Sev1/Sev2 the way you’re using it?

[00:01:58] Sean Gallagher: In our system, Sev1 is crash/data corruption/unavailability or major latency regression affecting a meaningful portion of traffic—like an SLO breach. Sev2 is degraded performance or functional bugs with a workaround. For LTS we bias toward only Sev1 plus security; Sev2 is case-by-case.

[00:02:21] Michael Chen: The latency regression point is a big one. We had a vendor last year where a “patch” was 12% p95 worse and they called it acceptable. For us, it’s not.

[00:02:33] Sean Gallagher: Yeah. For LTS we’re adding perf gates specifically. That’s part of why we’re standing this up as a separate channel. A patch cannot ship if it regresses beyond thresholds on our canonical workloads, unless there’s a documented waiver with a mitigation and sign-off.

[00:02:49] Sahana Iyer: And those gates run on release branches, not just main. That’s important, because we’ve seen some “only on release branch” issues when toolchain differs.

[00:03:00] Avery Johnson: Maybe we can structure this as: cadence and SLA, then perf gates, then artifact verification and pinning.

[00:03:08] Priya Nair: Works.

[00:03:10] Connor O'Brien: On cadence: the default is monthly patch cadence—say second Tuesday-ish, but we’ll commit to “one patch per month” rather than a calendar date at first. Out-of-band patches for security are separate.

[00:03:27] Jordan Miles: Out-of-band meaning you can ship whenever you want?

[00:03:31] Connor O'Brien: Only for security or “stop the bleeding” Sev1. We’d still produce release notes, signed artifacts, checksums. But the timeline is compressed.

[00:03:44] Elena Petrova: Okay, what’s the SLA on security? We have vendor risk requirements: critical CVE response in 72 hours or less typically.

[00:03:55] Sean Gallagher: We’re working toward targets like: confirmed critical vuln with exploitability—hotfix within 48 to 72 hours. But there are dependencies: upstream disclosures, customer-specific backports, etc.

[00:04:08] Connor O'Brien: For LTS we’re formalizing it, including who can approve an emergency patch, and what gates can be abbreviated versus not. Like, we still run correctness tests; perf gates might be a smaller subset with explicit “risk accepted” if we’re racing.

[00:04:26] Michael Chen: That’s the thing. We can accept abbreviated perf gates for a security patch if there’s a rollback plan and you tell us what’s being skipped.

[00:04:35] Connor O'Brien: Exactly. The patch notes would include a “verification performed” section and a “known risk / mitigations” section.

[00:04:44] Priya Nair: And for non-security, monthly patches, what’s the lead time? Like, when do we get RCs? We do CAB.

[00:04:54] Connor O'Brien: For steady state, we’re thinking: RC build about 5 business days before publish, with perf gate results attached. Then final tag after sign-offs.

[00:05:06] Jordan Miles: “Sign-offs” from who?

[00:05:09] Connor O'Brien: Release Eng plus runtime owners and SRE. For LTS we’re explicitly requiring SRE sign-off when there’s any risk to latency/availability.

[00:05:20] Elena Petrova: And security sign-off for security patches?

[00:05:23] Sean Gallagher: Yep, and vuln response coordinator. We can share the workflow in writing.

[00:05:30] Michael Chen: Backport SLA for Sev1: what do you commit to? Because “we’ll try” is not useful.

[00:05:37] Connor O'Brien: The draft target is: Sev1 with customer impact and a reproduction—triage within 1 business day, decision on LTS eligibility within 2 business days, and a patched release candidate within 7 calendar days if the fix is already in mainline and cherry-pickable.

[00:05:55] Priya Nair: “If already in mainline” is a big caveat.

[00:05:58] Connor O'Brien: True. If it’s not fixed yet anywhere, then it’s a normal bugfix timeline, but we’d prioritize based on severity. LTS doesn’t magically create engineering capacity, but it creates the process and the commitment to not ship risky changes.

[00:06:15] Sean Gallagher: And, practically, for most Sev1s we see in runtime, the fix lands quickly. The LTS question is: can we backport with minimal diff and low risk.

[00:06:26] Michael Chen: Can you elaborate on minimal diff? Like, do you avoid backporting refactors?

[00:06:32] Connor O'Brien: Yes. The rubric includes: isolate the change, avoid sweeping refactors, prefer targeted guards or small correctness fixes. If the fix depends on a big architectural change, we’d likely document a workaround for LTS and recommend moving to the next baseline, rather than destabilize.

[00:06:51] Priya Nair: That’s good, but it’s also scary, because it could mean we get stuck with a workaround for months.

[00:06:58] Connor O'Brien: That’s why we also define when we would cut a new LTS baseline. Like 1.18-lts is one line; later we may cut 1.20-lts or similar. But we’re not there yet.

[00:07:11] Avery Johnson: For the deal side, we can write this as: “Support window is 12 months for each LTS baseline; monthly patches; security OOB patches; and a documented backport policy.” We’ll get you the exact language.

[00:07:25] Jordan Miles: I’ll need that language, yes, and it needs to be consistent with what you’re saying here.

[00:07:31] Elena Petrova: Also artifact evidence. For us it’s not just “trust,” we need to verify what’s deployed matches what you shipped.

[00:07:39] Sahana Iyer: Yep. That’s my section.

[00:07:42] Connor O'Brien: Before artifacts, let’s do perf stability.

[00:07:46] Michael Chen: Please.

[00:07:48] Sean Gallagher: So for LTS, we run a perf canary suite—representative models and request mixes. We look at p50 and p95 latency, plus throughput tokens/sec, and GPU memory headroom. The gate is basically: no regression above thresholds relative to the previous LTS patch.

[00:08:07] Priya Nair: What thresholds? Like 5%?

[00:08:11] Sean Gallagher: For p95 we’re tightening to low single digits. Think ~3–5% depending on workload noise. Throughput similar. There’s also a “hard stop” if it’s worse than, say, 8–10% even if noise is suspected.

[00:08:26] Michael Chen: That’s more reasonable.

[00:08:29] Sean Gallagher: But there’s nuance. Benchmarks are noisy. So we run multiple trials and we tag the environment. If there’s variance due to runner contention, we rerun. We do not just waive because “it seems fine.”

[00:08:44] Connor O'Brien: And if we ever do waive, it’s explicit in the release notes: why, what we observed, and recommended mitigations, plus whether it’s “optional patch” or “strongly recommended.”

[00:08:57] Priya Nair: Wait—optional vs mandatory?

[00:09:00] Sean Gallagher: Right. Some patches are “mandatory” in the sense that they fix a severe issue and we’ll strongly encourage upgrade. But even then, we try to avoid forcing customers; we provide rollback guidance and we don’t auto-upgrade Private.

[00:09:15] Michael Chen: How do you handle the kernel side? Like, kernel autotune. That’s been a problem with other inference stacks—one patch changes heuristics and perf swings.

[00:09:26] Connor O'Brien: We’re freezing autotune defaults on the LTS branch. You can override, but the default behavior shouldn’t change patch-to-patch without an explicit “this will change perf” note.

[00:09:39] Priya Nair: That’s huge for us.

[00:09:41] Sean Gallagher: And similarly, we’re disabling experimental batching heuristics on LTS unless they’ve been proven stable. So you might see LTS slightly behind mainline peak throughput, but the goal is predictability.

[00:09:57] Michael Chen: I’m okay trading 3% throughput for stable p95.

[00:10:02] Elena Petrova: How does this relate to Dedicated vs Private? Dedicated is hosted by you, Private is on our infra.

[00:10:09] Avery Johnson: LTS is a runtime distribution choice across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private. Dedicated customers will likely choose LTS for predictability; Private can pin the same.

[00:10:20] Priya Nair: For Private, we need pinning at the patch level and a rollback without “surprises.” No pulling a different base image because a tag moved.

[00:10:30] Sahana Iyer: 100%. For LTS we treat tags as immutable. You’ll have a version like 1.18.1-lts, and the container digest is what you deploy. We’ll publish the digest, checksums for the artifact bundle, and a signature you can verify.

[00:10:46] Jordan Miles: Signature like GPG?

[00:10:49] Sahana Iyer: We’re using modern artifact signing—think Sigstore/cosign style—plus sha256 checksums. I don’t want to over-index on the specific tool in case naming changes, but the result is: you can verify the image digest matches the signed attestation.

[00:11:05] Elena Petrova: We need to know key management. Like, where are the keys stored, who can sign, and is it auditable.

[00:11:13] Sahana Iyer: Yes. Signing happens in CI with restricted permissions. Keys are stored in a managed KMS/HSM setup. Access is limited to release workflows, and we log signing events. We can provide audit evidence on request.

[00:11:29] Michael Chen: Can we verify offline? In our air-gapped-ish env, we can’t always hit public transparency logs.

[00:11:37] Sahana Iyer: Good call. For Private customers we can provide an offline verification path: you download the signature bundle and verify against published public keys, without needing outbound network to a transparency service. We’ve done this with other regulated accounts.

[00:11:54] Priya Nair: And SBOM?

[00:11:56] Sahana Iyer: We’re adding SBOM generation for LTS artifacts. It’s either attached to the release as a file or retrievable via the installer bundle. It will include component versions, and we’ll tie it to the exact commit/tag.

[00:12:12] Elena Petrova: That’s required. Also provenance, like SLSA attestation.

[00:12:17] Sahana Iyer: We have provenance metadata that maps artifact → git commit → build workflow. We can share an example of the attestation format.

[00:12:27] Connor O'Brien: And we’ll also connect it in the release notes: every patch has the list of backported PRs and the tag.

[00:12:36] Priya Nair: Release notes are another thing. We don’t want marketing fluff. We want: what changed, what risk, what known issues.

[00:12:44] Connor O'Brien: LTS notes are “slower-change” notes. Categories: security, reliability/correctness, performance changes, operational changes. Then known issues, upgrade notes, and compatibility matrix.

[00:12:58] Michael Chen: Compatibility matrix meaning?

[00:13:01] Connor O'Brien: Like: which CUDA driver ranges, which GPU SKUs we validate, which Kubernetes versions for Private if relevant, and which installer/Helm chart versions are compatible.

[00:13:13] Priya Nair: Good.

[00:13:15] Avery Johnson: Maybe we can talk about how you consume it: Private installer or Helm.

[00:13:21] Priya Nair: We’re a Helm shop for most clusters. Installer is okay for isolated envs.

[00:13:27] Sahana Iyer: Helm will support selecting LTS channel and pinning to a specific patch. The values file will accept “runtimeTag: 1.18.1-lts” and optionally the digest. We recommend pinning by digest for maximum immutability.

[00:13:44] Michael Chen: If we pin by digest, do we lose human readability?

[00:13:48] Sahana Iyer: You still keep the semantic tag in your config for readability, but the digest ensures it can’t drift. We can provide both in docs.

[00:13:59] Elena Petrova: How do you handle revoked or rotated keys? If a signing key is compromised.

[00:14:07] Sahana Iyer: Key rotation is planned and documented. If compromise occurs, we publish a security advisory, rotate keys, and re-sign as needed. For Private, you’d update trusted keys in your verification pipeline. We’ll provide a keyring and version it.

[00:14:23] Jordan Miles: We’ll need that as part of the vendor risk package.

[00:14:27] Avery Johnson: Yep, we’ll include it.

[00:14:30] Michael Chen: Back to perf gates. Are those representative of real workloads? Like mixed sequence lengths? Our traffic is bursty.

[00:14:40] Sean Gallagher: The suite includes mixed prompt lengths and concurrency patterns, including overload scenarios where scheduler behavior matters—queueing, backpressure, load shedding. That’s explicitly because those regress when you backport.

[00:14:57] Priya Nair: Do you test on the same GPUs we’d run?

[00:15:01] Sean Gallagher: We cover common SKUs—A100, H100, L40S. If you’re on something else, we can discuss. For Dedicated we control the fleet, for Private we try to align validation, but we can’t cover every on-prem variant.

[00:15:18] Michael Chen: We’re mostly A100 and some H100.

[00:15:22] Sean Gallagher: Great, that’s covered.

[00:15:24] Elena Petrova: Another requirement: auditable release artifacts. If an auditor asks “what code is running,” we must show traceability.

[00:15:33] Connor O'Brien: LTS patches will be traceable to a tag and commit, plus the provenance metadata. We’ll also keep release branch history intact—no rebases, no force pushes.

[00:15:46] Priya Nair: Thank you. Some vendors do force push release branches and it’s… yeah.

[00:15:51] Avery Johnson: Can we talk about support workflow? Like, if Northstar finds an issue and wants a backport.

[00:15:57] Michael Chen: Yes, what’s the path? We don’t want to open a ticket and have it disappear.

[00:16:03] Sean Gallagher: For LTS accounts we set an escalation path: Support intake captures severity, reproduction, logs, business impact, and whether it’s hosted/dedicated/private. Then Release Eng and runtime triage against the backport rubric. If it qualifies, it gets tracked into the next patch or an OOB patch.

[00:16:24] Connor O'Brien: And we’ll communicate patch availability proactively to your CS/TAM and to you directly. We’re trying to avoid “silent fixes.”

[00:16:36] Priya Nair: We’ll need a predictable communication cadence. Like release notes email and maybe a webhook.

[00:16:42] Avery Johnson: We can do email distribution and we have Console/Docs links. Webhook is on the roadmap but not GA yet.

[00:16:52] Michael Chen: As long as it’s not only in a UI.

[00:16:55] Avery Johnson: Agreed.

[00:16:57] Elena Petrova: One more thing: do you do data plane changes in LTS? Like, does the scheduler change behavior.

[00:17:06] Connor O'Brien: Only if it’s a fix for correctness or a Sev1 issue, and it passes regression tests. The default is “no behavior changes.” In practice, a bug fix could change behavior, but we’d highlight it.

[00:17:22] Michael Chen: Can you give an example of what would be backported?

[00:17:26] Connor O'Brien: Example: a KV cache compaction crash—Sev1—backport. Another: scheduler starvation edge case under bursty traffic—likely qualifies if reproducible and impacts availability.

[00:17:40] Sean Gallagher: And we’d add a regression test for it in the release branch suite.

[00:17:45] Priya Nair: Okay.

[00:17:47] Jordan Miles: I’m still fuzzy on “support window.” If we start on 1.18 LTS, when does it end?

[00:17:55] Connor O'Brien: Support window begins at LTS baseline cut. We’re targeting a 12-month maintenance period. At the end, we’ll provide an upgrade path to the next LTS baseline. We’ll give advance notice—like 90 days—before end of support.

[00:18:13] Jordan Miles: And is that in contract?

[00:18:16] Avery Johnson: We can put commitments into the order form / enterprise addendum. We’ll align with legal.

[00:18:22] Elena Petrova: For security, do you have CVE IDs? Like do you publish advisories.

[00:18:28] Sean Gallagher: We publish security advisories with CVE references when applicable, plus affected versions and mitigations. For Private we can share advisories under NDA if needed.

[00:18:41] Michael Chen: Okay. Another perf question: do you gate on customer traffic patterns? Like, our top model is… I can’t name it, but think 70B-ish.

[00:18:52] Sean Gallagher: We can add workload-specific perf validation during pilot. For the general LTS gate we use representative open models, but we can incorporate a similar shape. If you can share distribution—prompt length, output length, QPS—we can model it.

[00:19:09] Priya Nair: We can share ranges. Not exact prompts.

[00:19:13] Sean Gallagher: That’s fine.

[00:19:15] Sahana Iyer: Also for artifact verification in Private, if you have a CI pipeline that does image admission control—like verifying cosign—tell us what you use. We can provide step-by-step.

[00:19:28] Elena Petrova: We do admission policies, yes.

[00:19:31] Sahana Iyer: Great.

[00:19:33] Avery Johnson: Can we get into what “reproducible builds” means on our side? Sahana?

[00:19:38] Sahana Iyer: Sure. For LTS, we’re locking toolchain versions, base images, and dependency hashes. The build pipeline produces deterministic outputs as much as feasible. You’ll see that the artifact bundle includes: build manifest, git commit, dependency lock hashes, and checksums.

[00:19:58] Michael Chen: Deterministic container images are hard. Do you actually get byte-for-byte?

[00:20:03] Sahana Iyer: Not 100% byte-for-byte across all layers today, but we’re moving toward it. What we can guarantee is traceability and integrity: signed digest and provenance. That’s what auditors care about most.

[00:20:18] Elena Petrova: That’s acceptable if you can show the chain: commit → workflow → artifact digest.

[00:20:25] Sahana Iyer: Yes.

[00:20:27] Priya Nair: What about Helm chart compatibility? If we pin runtime 1.18.1-lts, do we also have to pin chart versions.

[00:20:36] Connor O'Brien: There will be a compatibility matrix. Our goal is: chart versions within a certain range work with the LTS runtime patches. We won’t require chart upgrades every patch.

[00:20:49] Michael Chen: Good.

[00:20:51] Priya Nair: And rollback: if a patch causes issues, can we roll back to previous patch quickly?

[00:20:58] Sean Gallagher: Yes. For Private, rollback is “deploy previous image digest/tag.” For Dedicated, we can help orchestrate rollback in your dedicated pool. We’ll also include rollback notes in release notes if there are schema changes or config changes.

[00:21:15] Michael Chen: “Schema changes” meaning what? Like metrics names?

[00:21:19] Sean Gallagher: Metrics, configs, sometimes cache formats. But in LTS, we try to avoid format changes. If unavoidable, we’d flag it and provide a safe migration plan.

[00:21:33] Elena Petrova: Can you commit to not changing logging formats? Our SIEM parsers break.

[00:21:39] Connor O'Brien: We can commit to stability of key log fields within LTS, and if any change is necessary, it’s documented as a breaking change. But the intent is “no churn.”

[00:21:52] Priya Nair: Okay.

[00:21:53] Avery Johnson: We’re around the halfway point. Anything else you want to drill into?

[00:21:59] Michael Chen: I want to understand the waiver process. If perf gate fails, who decides to ship anyway.

[00:22:06] Sean Gallagher: Waivers require Release Eng and SRE approval at minimum, and usually runtime owner. The waiver doc includes: benchmark data, explanation of noise vs real regression, customer impact analysis, and mitigation—like config toggles.

[00:22:24] Connor O'Brien: And in LTS, waivers should be rare. If we can’t prove it’s noise or acceptable, we hold the patch.

[00:22:33] Michael Chen: That’s what we want to hear.

[00:22:35] Priya Nair: How do you handle “perf improvements” in LTS? Do you backport them?

[00:22:42] Connor O'Brien: Usually no, unless it also fixes a Sev1 and it’s low risk. Perf-only changes are a big source of instability. We’d rather keep LTS stable.

[00:22:54] Priya Nair: So LTS might not get the newest throughput improvements.

[00:22:58] Connor O'Brien: Correct. That’s the trade.

[00:23:02] Jordan Miles: That’s actually easier for us, because it reduces change control events.

[00:23:07] Elena Petrova: One more security detail: vulnerability scanning. Do you scan images and share results?

[00:23:14] Sahana Iyer: We do scanning as part of CI. For LTS releases we can include a summary—like “no critical vulnerabilities detected at build time,” plus list of known accepted risks if any.

[00:23:27] Elena Petrova: We’ll likely rescan ourselves, but having your scan report helps.

[00:23:32] Sahana Iyer: Yep.

[00:23:34] Michael Chen: On the runtime branch: is it literally a separate branch, or just tags.

[00:23:41] Connor O'Brien: Separate branch, like runtime-1.18-lts. That branch only takes cherry-picked backports. Tags for each patch, like 1.18.1-lts.

[00:23:55] Priya Nair: That’s good. We can audit branch diffs.

[00:24:00] Connor O'Brien: Exactly. And it makes it easier to reason about what changed.

[00:24:06] Avery Johnson: Minor point, but you asked earlier about competitors—some customers compare this to vLLM stable releases or cloud providers. Our goal is to make this enterprise-friendly.

[00:24:18] Priya Nair: Yeah, we’ve looked at Bedrock, but Private requirements and control are hard.

[00:24:24] Michael Chen: And Azure OpenAI is not an option for certain data.

[00:24:28] Avery Johnson: Understood.

[00:24:30] Elena Petrova: For Private, do you support customer-managed KMS and audit logs.

[00:24:36] Avery Johnson: Yes, though that’s more the platform layer. For runtime LTS, we ensure artifacts and release process are auditable. For Private deployment, you can integrate with KMS/HSM for keys and we provide audit logs.

[00:24:51] Priya Nair: Okay.

[00:24:53] Michael Chen: Let’s talk incident-driven backports. If we have an incident and it’s a Sev1, do we get priority, or is it “best effort.”

[00:25:02] Sean Gallagher: LTS accounts have an escalation path. Priority is based on severity and breadth of impact. If it’s your incident only but Sev1, it’s still top priority. We’ll also look if other customers are affected.

[00:25:17] Connor O'Brien: The key is: we can’t promise a fix in X days for every possible unknown bug, but we can promise the workflow, explicit decisions, and transparent status.

[00:25:30] Priya Nair: That transparency matters. We don’t want to chase updates.

[00:25:34] Avery Johnson: Makes sense.

[00:25:36] Elena Petrova: How do you communicate security hotfixes? Do you email, do you post, what.

[00:25:43] Sean Gallagher: For enterprise, direct notification through your support channel + advisory. For Hosted/Dedicated we can also show in Console. For Private, we send release notes and artifact pointers.

[00:25:58] Jordan Miles: We’ll need those notifications to go to a shared mailbox, not an individual.

[00:26:03] Avery Johnson: Noted.

[00:26:05] Priya Nair: This all sounds promising. But we want proof. Can you show an example of what an LTS patch release bundle looks like?

[00:26:14] Sahana Iyer: We can send a sample for a candidate patch—includes: release notes, image digest, checksums file, signature, provenance attestation, SBOM. We can sanitize if needed.

[00:26:29] Elena Petrova: Please include verification commands. Our security review likes “copy/paste.”

[00:26:35] Sahana Iyer: Yep.

[00:26:37] Michael Chen: And perf gate output. Like, a report.

[00:26:41] Sean Gallagher: We can share a summary report—p50/p95, throughput, and the run environment. Maybe not raw logs, but the key results.

[00:26:52] Priya Nair: That’s enough.

[00:26:54] Avery Johnson: We’re at ~27 minutes. Anything else you want to cover before we recap.

[00:27:00] Priya Nair: One more question: if we choose LTS, can we still use Dedicated autoscaling and the newer console features.

[00:27:08] Avery Johnson: Yes. LTS is about runtime stability. Console features are separate; you can get observability improvements without changing runtime.

[00:27:17] Michael Chen: As long as console doesn’t force a runtime update.

[00:27:20] Avery Johnson: It won’t.

[00:27:22] Connor O'Brien: Also for LTS release notes, we’ll include a “no action required” section if a patch is purely security with no config changes.

[00:27:33] Priya Nair: Good.

[00:27:35] Elena Petrova: And do you do FIPS builds?

[00:27:38] Avery Johnson: For certain Private deployments we can discuss. It’s not strictly in LTS scope, but we have compliance packages. We can follow up separately.

[00:27:49] Elena Petrova: Okay.

[00:27:51] Michael Chen: I think we’re good.

[00:27:53] Avery Johnson: Alright, recap and next steps.

[00:27:57] Avery Johnson: One: we’ll share draft LTS policy language—12-month support window, monthly patch cadence, security hotfix procedure, and backport rubric.

[00:28:07] Avery Johnson: Two: Sahana will provide an example artifact bundle with checksums, signature verification steps, provenance, and SBOM.

[00:28:15] Avery Johnson: Three: Sean will send a sample perf gate summary and the thresholds we’re targeting for p95 and throughput, plus how waivers work.

[00:28:25] Avery Johnson: Four: Priya/Michael, you’ll send your CAB/change-control constraints—blackout windows, lead times, and your severity mapping.

[00:28:36] Priya Nair: Yes.

[00:28:37] Michael Chen: Yep.

[00:28:38] Elena Petrova: And I’ll send our signing/offline verification requirements. The air-gapped question.

[00:28:44] Sahana Iyer: Perfect.

[00:28:46] Jordan Miles: And I’ll need the commitment language for procurement. Please don’t bury it in a technical doc.

[00:28:52] Avery Johnson: Understood—separate one-pager.

[00:28:56] Connor O'Brien: Thanks all. We’ll follow up in email with the materials.

[00:29:01] Priya Nair: Thanks.

[00:29:03] Michael Chen: Thank you.

[00:29:04] Elena Petrova: Thanks.

[00:29:05] Avery Johnson: Alright, stopping recording.

---
Note: Timestamps after ~29 minutes are truncated in the export; recording duration shows 55 minutes but the remainder was largely Q&A while screensharing a draft checklist and canary report. Key points from the second half: Northstar reiterated need for offline signature verification, and requested that LTS release notes always include: (a) exact image digests, (b) “tests run” checklist, and (c) explicit statement whether any perf waivers were used.

title: Northstar Bank — Runtime LTS technical deep dive (cadence, backports, perf gates, artifacts)
preview: Northstar platform/SRE/security team asked for concrete Runtime LTS commitments: 12-month support window expectations, monthly patch cadence + out-of-band security hotfixes, backport eligibility and SLA, performance regression gating and waiver handling, and how Redwood provides verifiable artifacts (checksums/signatures/provenance/SBOM) for Private deployments. Redwood outlined proposed runtime-1.18-lts branch model, risk rubric for backports, perf canary gates (p50/p95 latency + throughput), and artifact verification workflow tied to commits and release notes. Customer requested written language for vendor risk and a sample signed artifact + SBOM.
text: Northstar platform/SRE/security team asked for concrete Runtime LTS commitments: 12-month support window expectations, monthly patch cadence + out-of-band security hotfixes, backport eligibility and SLA, performance regression gating and waiver handling, and how Redwood provides verifiable artifacts (checksums/signatures/provenance/SBOM) for Private deployments. Redwood outlined proposed runtime-1.18-lts branch model, risk rubric for backports, perf canary gates (p50/p95 latency + throughput), and artifact verification workflow tied to commits and release notes. Customer requested written language for vendor risk and a sample signed artifact + SBOM.
